Article.ArticleTitle: Weight Gain Recommendations for Athletes and Military Personnel: a Critical Review of the Evidence.

Abstract.AbstractText: Sports nutrition guidelines typically state that athletes desiring weight gain follow a regimen that includes increasing energy intake by ~ 300-500 kcal/day with an emphasis on adequate protein and carbohydrate and judicious inclusion of energy-dense foods, in combination with rigorous resistance training. This regimen is thought to promote weekly gains of ~ 0.45 kg (1 lb), mostly as lean body mass (LBM). This review summarizes the evidence supporting these intentional weight gain regimens in athletes.

Although some research has been conducted in the past 5 years, research on intentional weight gain is lacking. Currently, available data suggests that weekly weight gain of 0.45 kg (1 lb), primarily as LBM, may be difficult for some athletes to achieve. Available evidence, however, suggests that commonly recommended strategies to promote calorie surplus, including consuming larger portions, incorporating energy-dense foods, and prioritizing liquid over solid foods, may prove helpful.
Abstract.CopyrightInformation: © 2022. The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Science+Business Media, LLC, part of Springer Nature.

ReferenceList.Title: Papers of particular interest, published recently, have been highlighted as: • Of importance •• Of major importance

A systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression of the effect of protein supplementation on resistance training-induced gains in muscle mass and strength in healthy adults. Br J Sports Med. 2018;52(6):376–84. https://doi.org/10.1136/bjsports-2017-097608 .

Stokes T, Hector AJ, Morton RW, McGlory C, Phillips SM. Recent perspectives regarding the role of dietary protein for the promotion of muscle hypertrophy with resistance exercise training. Nutrients. 2018;10(2). https://doi.org/10.3390/nu10020180 .

• Minnick C, Raffoul A, Hammond D, Kirkpatrick SI. Intentional weight gain efforts among young Canadian adults aged 17–32 years. Eat Behav. 2020;38: 101407. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eatbeh.2020.101407 . This survey of a sample of over 900,000 young Canadian adults shows that a significant number of both men and women are intentionally attempting weight gain. While it is not known how many of these young adults are engaged in sport, it highlights the importance of gaining a better understanding of healthy weight gain.

Garthe I, Raastad T, Sundgot-Borgen J. Long-term effect of nutritional counselling on desired gain in body mass and lean body mass in elite athletes. Appl Physiol Nutr Metab. 2011;36(4):547–54. https://doi.org/10.1139/h11-051 .

J Acad Nutr Diet. 2021. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jand.2021.01.011 .

Spillane M, Willoughby DS. Daily overfeeding from protein and/or carbohydrate supplementation for eight weeks in conjunction with resistance training does not improve body composition and muscle strength or increase markers indicative of muscle protein synthesis and myogenesis in resistance-trained males. J Sports Sci Med. 2016;15(1):17–25.

Nagasawa T, Murata H, Muraoka Y, Natsui H, Taguchi M. Study of new approaches to increase the body weight of Japanese athletes. Journal of Japanese Society of Clinical Sports Medicine. 2013;21(2),:422–30.

•• Taguchi M, Hara A, Murata H, Torii S, Sako T. Increasing meal frequency in isoenergetic conditions does not affect body composition change and appetite during weight gain in japanese athletes. Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab. 2020;31(2):109–14. https://doi.org/10.1123/ijsnem.2020-0139 . This well-controlled dietary intervention in ten male collegiate rowers demonstrated the effectiveness of hypercaloric feeding combined with regular intense training on gains of both total body mass and fat mass. The study also shows that total calorie intake is more important than meals frequency, as long as total energy intake is held consistent. It is one of only a few handfuls of weight gain intervention studies conducted in athletes.

Sports (Basel). 2021;9(2). https://doi.org/10.3390/sports9020032 . This careful examination of the literature highlights that hypertrophic gains in muscle are not limited to moderate loads of ~8 to 10 reps on the repititum continuum.
